Devil May Cry 3 Special Edition Speedrun World Record Progression 2019-Present

Time Series Forecast

BanHitGaming

12/03/2026

Introduction

Devil May Cry 3: Dante’s Awakening (DMC 3) is a PlayStation 2 video game originally released in 2005, developed and published by Capcom. It was later re-released in 2006 as Devil May Cry 3: Dante’s Awakening Special Edition (DMC 3 SE) on the PlayStation 2 and is the most common version of the game ported on to modern systems and digital store fronts today as it fixes and adjusts the difficulty problem in the original release and balancing other gameplay elements.

Speedrunning in its simplest terms is the act of playing a video game as fast as possible. It is very popular online with multiple leaderboards on the website Speedrun.com for various games to see who can get the best time!

This project will be focusing on the history, progression and forecasting the World Record (WR) for the DMC 3 SE speedrun category New Game (NG) Dante PC using Meta’s Prophet model in R.

The dataset used was made from scratch by myself referencing the history tab on the DMC 3 SE Leaderboard (where I got my data to put into my csv file), the DMC Speedrun Wiki and DMC 3 SE World Record History video made by WaifuRuns. This will also only be covering the World Record from 2019 to present day.

Devil May Cry 3: Dante’s Awakening Special Edition

Dataset Preparation

The dataset will be loaded up and inspected to ensure everything is in working order along with the packages prophet, which will ensure we have access to the correct packages to produce our forecast and time series, and hms, which ensures our time duration data is easily handled by converting the hh:mm:ss into seconds.

library(prophet)
## Warning: package 'prophet' was built under R version 4.3.3
## Loading required package: Rcpp
## Warning: package 'Rcpp' was built under R version 4.3.3
## Loading required package: rlang
## Warning: package 'rlang' was built under R version 4.3.3
library(hms)

dataDMC3SE = read.csv(file.path("data","DMC3SE WR History 2019-2026 - WR History.csv"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
dataDMC3SE
##        Username    Time       Date Difference.Between.Runs
## 1      Simoteus 1:03:50 2019-10-06                        
## 2     LonerHero 1:01:53 2021-03-15                -0:01:57
## 3         Waifu 1:01:43 2021-07-08                -0:00:10
## 4         Waifu 0:59:31 2021-07-11                -0:02:12
## 5      Simoteus 0:58:55 2021-09-26                -0:00:36
## 6  esfrenchie09 0:58:09 2021-10-11                -0:00:46
## 7  esfrenchie09 0:58:05 2021-11-17                -0:00:04
## 8  esfrenchie09 0:57:39 2022-01-22                -0:00:26
## 9      Simoteus 0:57:13 2022-01-29                -0:00:26
## 10     Simoteus 0:56:51 2022-05-27                -0:00:22
## 11     Simoteus 0:56:32 2022-12-30                -0:00:19
## 12    LonerHero 0:56:16 2023-04-06                -0:00:16
## 13     Simoteus 0:56:07 2024-02-09                -0:00:09
## 14        Waifu 0:56:04 2025-02-05                -0:00:03
## 15        Stuby 0:55:33 2025-03-29                -0:00:31
## 16        Stuby 0:54:39 2025-04-11                -0:00:54
## 17        Waifu 0:54:34 2025-04-18                -0:00:05
## 18       jSlash 0:53:49 2025-08-13                -0:00:45
## 19       jSlash 0:53:13 2026-01-04                -0:00:36
str(dataDMC3SE)
## 'data.frame':    19 obs. of  4 variables:
##  $ Username               : chr  "Simoteus" "LonerHero" "Waifu" "Waifu" ...
##  $ Time                   : chr  "1:03:50" "1:01:53" "1:01:43" "0:59:31" ...
##  $ Date                   : chr  "2019-10-06" "2021-03-15" "2021-07-08" "2021-07-11" ...
##  $ Difference.Between.Runs: chr  "" "-0:01:57" "-0:00:10" "-0:02:12" ...
head(dataDMC3SE)
##       Username    Time       Date Difference.Between.Runs
## 1     Simoteus 1:03:50 2019-10-06                        
## 2    LonerHero 1:01:53 2021-03-15                -0:01:57
## 3        Waifu 1:01:43 2021-07-08                -0:00:10
## 4        Waifu 0:59:31 2021-07-11                -0:02:12
## 5     Simoteus 0:58:55 2021-09-26                -0:00:36
## 6 esfrenchie09 0:58:09 2021-10-11                -0:00:46
names(dataDMC3SE)
## [1] "Username"                "Time"                   
## [3] "Date"                    "Difference.Between.Runs"
summary(dataDMC3SE)
##    Username             Time               Date          
##  Length:19          Length:19          Length:19         
##  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
##  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
##  Difference.Between.Runs
##  Length:19              
##  Class :character       
##  Mode  :character

The dataset contains 4 columns: Username, name of the speedrunner, Time, the time duration they got for that WR, Date, the date that it happened, and Difference Between Runs, the difference in time between the previous WRs. Prophet requires the data to be stored in a dataframe with columns ds and y, ds will contain the dates and y will contain the numerical values to forecast.

dataDMC3SE$Date = as.Date(dataDMC3SE$Date)
dataDMC3SE$Time = as.numeric(hms::as_hms(dataDMC3SE$Time))

wr_data = data.frame(ds = dataDMC3SE$Date, y = dataDMC3SE$Time)
head(wr_data)
##           ds    y
## 1 2019-10-06 3830
## 2 2021-03-15 3713
## 3 2021-07-08 3703
## 4 2021-07-11 3571
## 5 2021-09-26 3535
## 6 2021-10-11 3489
summary(wr_data)
##        ds                   y       
##  Min.   :2019-10-06   Min.   :3193  
##  1st Qu.:2021-10-03   1st Qu.:3348  
##  Median :2022-05-27   Median :3411  
##  Mean   :2023-02-04   Mean   :3444  
##  3rd Qu.:2025-03-03   3rd Qu.:3512  
##  Max.   :2026-01-04   Max.   :3830

Analysing The Data

We will first plot the time series of the data before making a forecast. Since we are working with time duration, I had to ensure R was reading the Time column correctly by make the values numeric and in seconds, then I ensured that the y-axis on the plot displayed the time duration in the form HH:MM:SS (hours:mins:seconds). The x-axis will display the date in years.

plot(wr_data$ds, wr_data$y,
     type = "o",
     col = "red",
     pch = 19,
     lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Date",
     ylab = "Time HH:MM:SS",
     yaxt = "n",
     main = "DMC 3 SE WR Progression" )
ticks = pretty(wr_data$y)
axis(2, at = ticks, labels = format(hms::as_hms(ticks)))

print(wr_data)
##            ds    y
## 1  2019-10-06 3830
## 2  2021-03-15 3713
## 3  2021-07-08 3703
## 4  2021-07-11 3571
## 5  2021-09-26 3535
## 6  2021-10-11 3489
## 7  2021-11-17 3485
## 8  2022-01-22 3459
## 9  2022-01-29 3433
## 10 2022-05-27 3411
## 11 2022-12-30 3392
## 12 2023-04-06 3376
## 13 2024-02-09 3367
## 14 2025-02-05 3364
## 15 2025-03-29 3333
## 16 2025-04-11 3279
## 17 2025-04-18 3274
## 18 2025-08-13 3229
## 19 2026-01-04 3193

The plot and data above shows a general decrease in the WR time, with it remaining the same from 2024 to 2025, then decreasing again. One thing to point out is the frequency of the plot points. From 2021 onwards there has been a large frequency in the amount of WRs submitted. This is due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and with everyone stuck indoors, it gave a lot of people free time to discover speedrunning and to learn the run. It also wasn’t until March 2021 when the runner LonerHero would consistently put in the work, rise up the ranks, and beat Simoteus’ WR at the time, which he had held for almost 3 years. This achievement would influence and inspire a lot of other people to pick up and learn the game. With new strategies and routes being made, (including a strategy I discovered myself), the game was whittled down to being under an hour, which was a huge milestone for the community, with WaifuRuns being the first to achieve this! Another piece of information to bring up is that there was a community member who was also heavily involved in this WR history, but he has now since then deleted anything he has submitted and therefore not included in this data.

Checking Trends With Linear Regression

model = lm(wr_data$y ~ wr_data$ds, data=wr_data)
plot(wr_data$ds, wr_data$y,
     type = "o",
     col = "red",
     pch = 19,
     lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Date",
     ylab = "Time HH:MM:SS",
     yaxt = "n",
     main = "DMC 3 SE WR Progression" )
ticks = pretty(wr_data$y)
axis(2, at = ticks, labels = format(hms::as_hms(ticks)))
abline(model, col = "blue", lwd = 2)

summary(model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wr_data$y ~ wr_data$ds, data = wr_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -95.686 -60.360  -5.519  41.768 127.638 
## 
## Coefficients:
##               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 7859.46234  494.66877  15.888 1.24e-11 ***
## wr_data$ds    -0.22768    0.02549  -8.931 7.90e-08 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 72.98 on 17 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.8243, Adjusted R-squared:  0.814 
## F-statistic: 79.77 on 1 and 17 DF,  p-value: 7.901e-08

The slope coefficient of -0.22768 means on average that the DMC 3 SE WR roughly decreases by 0.22768 seconds a day and confirms the downward trend of the plot. The p-value of 7.901e-08 is very small and implies that the downward trend is significant and not likely be due to random variation. (Although random elements are also to be factored in the DMC 3 SE Speedrun, it is mostly skill based and execution heavy.) The Multiple R-Squared value of 0.8243 suggests that the linear trend explains roughly 82.43% of the variation WR Progression, a value this high implies the trend captures most of the movement of the data.

Prophet Forecasting Model

wr_model = prophet(wr_data,
                   yearly.seasonality = FALSE,
                   weekly.seasonality = FALSE,
                   daily.seasonality = FALSE)
## n.changepoints greater than number of observations. Using 14

I will be using yearly observations to forecast the dataset, the seasonal components will be turned off and modelling the trend will be the main focus.

Forecasting Future Speedruns

I will be forecasting the series forward by 4 years to predict the WR by 2030. This is to keep things realistic since the speedruns are being performed by humans, and there is also a limit to how far we can push the game.

future_wr = make_future_dataframe(wr_model, periods = 5, freq = "year")
forecast = predict(wr_model, future_wr)

Forecast Plot

plot(wr_model, forecast,
     xlab = "Date",
     ylab = "Time (seconds)")

This is the Prophet forecast for the DMC 3 SE WR Progression. The black dots are from the dataset, the blue line shows the model’s estimated trend and future predictions. The shaded area represents the uncertainty interval, this is a range where future WRs are likely to happen.

The forecast suggests a steady downward trend over the next 4 years as suggested in the data. The uncertainty area gets wider into the future showing that the prediction becomes less certain as the forecast progresses.

Further Discussion

This goes without saying that this should not be used as a definitive way to predict the future WR of this speedrun, as there are many factors that can influence when a new WR has been recorded such as how many active runners are playing the game at a single moment, which is not included in this data.

Another piece of information I want to mention is that speedrunners use a tool called LiveSplit, which keeps track of their run time, and individual times in a segment of a run. The best times of each segment in the run is saved and over time is added up to make a “Sum of Best”, which is not included in this data set. The Sum of Best (SoB) shows the theoretical best time for a speedrunner and for some of these runners, their SoB is around the 00:50:xx mark. It is definitely doable, it is now only a matter of when will it happen. With the top 5 consisting of all previous WR holders, will it be them or someone new who will take it?

Top 5 Leaderboard as of 17.3.2026

Conclusion

The DMC 3 SE speedrun has come a long way, with the current WR being around 10 minutes faster than what it was in 2019, and it will continue to to follow that downward trend. This can be certain as speedrunning is an ever-evolving practice that constantly aims to get the lowest time as possible. The linear regression model confirms that the downward trend is not due to random variation and is significant. Using the Prophet forecasting, it is predicted by 2030 that the speedrun will be close to 3000 seconds (50 minutes), but as mentioned prior, SoB shows there is a possibility to reach that time if the run has little to no flaws, so there could be a possibility someone gets that time earlier than what Prophet anticipates.

I would like to thank the DMC Speedrun Community for making this project possible, and I would like to thank WaifuRuns for making his informative video on the WR history.

  • DMC 3 SE Speedrun Leaderboard: https://www.speedrun.com/dmc3se
